questions.text: Adult human RBCs are anucleate. Which of the following statement(s) is/are the most appropriate explanation for this feature? (1) They do not need to reproduce. (2) They are somatic cells. (3) They do not metabolize. (4) All their internal space is available for oxygen transport.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because while Red blood cells do not need to reproduce, this alone does not justify their anucleate nature. Other factors contribute to this feature.
This option is incorrect because while factors (1) and (4) are relevant, factor (3) is incorrect. RBCs do metabolize, though limited, through glycolysis.

This option is incorrect because being somatic cells (2) does not specifically explain the absence of a nucleus. While their metabolism is limited, (3) is misleading as RBCs do metabolize anaerobically.
This is the correct option because the absence of a nucleus allows for maximum space to be dedicated to oxygen transport by accommodating more hemoglobin.

questions.explanation: Red blood cells (RBCs) in adult humans lack a nucleus and other organelles, which provides more internal space for hemoglobin, the oxygen-carrying molecule. This adaptation maximizes RBCs' oxygen transport capacity. Option (4) is correct because it directly correlates the anucleate nature of RBCs to their primary function of oxygen transport. Other options are incorrect because factors like reproduction (1) and being somatic cells (2) are not directly related to the absence of a nucleus, and RBCs do have a limited metabolism (3), relying on anaerobic mechanisms.

questions.text: Which one of the following animals has two separate circulatory pathways?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The blood vessels of the body are functionally divided into two distinctive circuits: the pulmonary circuit and the systemic circuit. A whale is a mammal that has two separate circulatory pathways.

Sharks are cartilaginous fish, and their circulatory system is different from mammals and other bony fish. Sharks have a single-circuit circulatory system, which means that blood flows through the heart and circulates through the body in one continuous loop. Deoxygenated blood is pumped from the heart to the gills, where it is oxygenated, and then it is distributed to the rest of the body before returning to the heart.
Frogs are amphibians, and they have a mixed circulatory system that changes throughout their life cycle.
Lizards are reptiles and have a double-circuit circulatory system similar to mammals.

questions.explanation: Whale is a mammal and in mammals, two separate circulatory pathways are found — systemic circulation and pulmonary circulation. Oxygenated and deoxygenated bloods received by the left and right atria respectively, pass on to the left and right ventricles. Thus, oxygenated and deoxygenated bloods are not mixed. This is referred to as double circulation. So A is the correct option.

questions.text: The given figure shows schematic plan of blood circulation in humans with labels A to D. Identify the label and give its functions?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: C ­-Vena cava- takes blood from body parts to right auricle, pCO 2 = 45 mm Hg
options.explanation: The vena cava is a large vein in humans that carries deoxygenated blood back to the heart. There are two main types: the superior vena cava and the inferior vena cava. The superior vena cava collects deoxygenated blood from the upper body, including the head, neck, and arms, and delivers it to the right atrium of the heart. The inferior vena cava collects deoxygenated blood from the lower body, including the abdomen, pelvis, and legs, and also delivers it to the right atrium. The vena cava plays a crucial role in returning blood to the heart for oxygenation and circulation throughout the body.

options.text: D-Dorsal aorta-­ takes blood from heart to body parts, pO 2 = 95 mm Hg
options.explanation: D is the pulmonary artery. The pulmonary artery in humans is a large blood vessel that carries deoxygenated blood from the heart to the lungs. It is the only artery in the body that carries deoxygenated blood. The main function of the pulmonary artery is to transport blood from the right ventricle of the heart to the lungs, where it undergoes oxygenation. Once oxygenated, the blood returns to the heart via the pulmonary veins and is then pumped out to the rest of the body through the aorta.

options.text: A-Pulmonary vein - takes impure blood from body parts, pO 2 = 60 mm Hg
options.explanation: The pulmonary vein is a blood vessel that carries oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the heart. It is the only vein in the body that carries oxygen-rich blood. The pulmonary vein receives blood from the pulmonary capillaries in the lungs, where oxygen is exchanged for carbon dioxide. It then transports this oxygenated blood to the left atrium of the heart, where it is subsequently pumped to the rest of the body to supply oxygen to tissues and organs. The pulmonary vein plays a crucial role in the circulation of oxygenated blood within the cardiovascular system.

options.text: B-Pulmonary artery takes blood from heart to lungs, pO2 = 90 mm Hg
options.explanation: B is the dorsal aorta. The dorsal aorta is a major blood vessel in humans that originates from the heart's left ventricle and extends down the backside of the body. It serves as the main arterial pathway, supplying oxygenated blood to various organs and tissues throughout the body. The dorsal aorta branches into smaller arteries, which further divide into capillaries to facilitate the exchange of nutrients, oxygen, and waste products between the blood and tissues. The dorsal aorta plays a crucial role in maintaining proper blood circulation and supporting the overall functioning of the human body.

questions.text: The figure shows a human blood cell. Identify it and give its characteristics.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Basophil - Involved in inflammatory response and allergic reactions
options.explanation: Basophils are white blood cells with large, irregular shapes and dark-staining granules in their cytoplasm. These granules contain histamine and other inflammatory mediators. Upon encountering an allergen, basophils release these substances, playing a key role in allergic reactions and inflammation.

options.text: B-lymphocyte - Key player in adaptive immune response, produces antibodies
options.explanation: B-lymphocytes are characterized by their large nuclei and minimal cytoplasm. They play a vital role in the adaptive immune system by producing antibodies in response to specific antigens, thus marking them for destruction by other immune cells.

options.text: Neutrophil - Most abundant white blood cell, essential for phagocytosis
options.explanation: Neutrophils have a multi-lobed nucleus and granular cytoplasm. They are the most prevalent white blood cells and are crucial for the innate immune response, acting as the first line of defense through phagocytosis of bacteria and fungi.

options.text: Monocyte - Large cells that differentiate into macrophages or dendritic cells
options.explanation: Monocytes are large cells with a kidney-shaped nucleus. They act as phagocytes and can differentiate into macrophages or dendritic cells in tissues, contributing to the body's immune defense by presenting antigens to T cells.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Basophil. Basophils are white blood cells that contain granules filled with histamine and other substances. They play a significant role in allergic reactions and inflammation by releasing these substances, which dilate blood vessels and increase vascular permeability. The other options are incorrect for the following reasons: B-lymphocytes are primarily involved in producing antibodies as part of the adaptive immune system, not in releasing inflammatory mediators. Neutrophils are phagocytic cells that are the most abundant white blood cells, focusing on engulfing pathogens rather than releasing histamine. Finally, Monocytes are large phagocytic cells that differentiate into macrophages or dendritic cells in tissues, and their primary function is not related to histamine release or immediate inflammatory responses.

questions.text: Which one of the following statements is correct regarding blood pressure?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A blood pressure reading of 130/90 mm Hg is classified as elevated, but it does not automatically necessitate treatment. Management may involve lifestyle changes, and treatment should be discussed with a healthcare provider.

This statement is misleading. While lower blood pressure can be associated with good health, 100/55 mm Hg might be too low for some individuals. Ideal blood pressure varies based on personal health factors.
Blood pressure readings do not determine a person's activity level. Activity can be influenced by various factors, and blood pressure can change during physical exertion.

options.text: 190/110 mm Hg may harm vital organs
options.explanation: A reading of 190/110 mm Hg indicates severe hypertension, which can lead to serious health issues, including damage to critical organs such as the brain and kidneys.

questions.explanation: Severe hypertension (≥180/110 mmHg) damages kidneys, brain, and heart, making it life-threatening.

questions.text: The fastest distribution of some injectable material/medicine and with no risk of any kind, can be achieved by injecting it into the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, the fastest distribution of some injectable material/medicine and with no risk of any kind cannot be achieved by injecting it into capillaries. Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels in the body. They are located in the tissues and are responsible for the exchange of oxygen and nutrients between the blood and the cells. Injecting material into capillaries would not result in the fastest distribution of the material. This is because the capillaries are very small and the material would have to travel a long distance to reach the cells.

No, the fastest distribution of some injectable material/medicine and with no risk of any kind cannot be achieved by injecting it into arteries. The arteries are the blood vessels that carry blood away from the heart. They are very high-pressure vessels, and injecting material into them can be dangerous. The material could be forced into the wrong parts of the body, or it could damage the arteries themselves.

Yes, the fastest distribution of an injectable material or medicine is achieved by injecting it into the veins. This is because veins are blood vessels that carry blood back to the heart. The blood in the veins is under pressure, which helps to push the medicine throughout the body quickly. Veins are blood vessels that carry blood back to the heart. They are typically thin-walled and have valves that prevent blood from flowing backwards.

No, the fastest distribution of some injectable material/medicine and with no risk of any kind cannot be achieved by injecting it into the lymph vessels. The lymph vessels are part of the lymphatic system, which is a network of vessels that carry lymph fluid throughout the body. Lymph fluid is a clear, watery fluid that contains white blood cells, proteins, and other substances.The lymph vessels are not directly connected to the bloodstream. This means that any medicine that is injected into the lymph vessels will have to travel through the lymphatic system before it can reach the bloodstream.

(ii) Angina is acute chest pain when the blood circulation to the brain is reduced. (iii) Persons with blood group AB can donate blood to any person with any blood group under ABO system. Calcium ions are essential for the activation of clotting factors and the formation of a stable blood clot.

questions.text: In a standard ECG, which one of the following alphabets is the correct representation of the respective activity of the human heart?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The P wave does not indicate the start of systole. It represents atrial depolarization, which occurs before the ventricles contract. Systole is associated with the QRS complex.
The T wave signifies ventricular repolarization, not the end of diastole. Diastole is the period of relaxation and filling of the heart chambers, following systole.
The P wave in an ECG represents the depolarization of the atria, which is the electrical activity causing the atria to contract.
The R wave is part of the QRS complex, representing ventricular depolarization. Ventricular repolarization is indicated by the T wave.

questions.explanation: The P wave in a standard ECG represents the depolarization of the atria. This marks the electrical activity that causes the atria to contract and push blood into the ventricles. The P wave is a small, rounded wave at the beginning of the ECG tracing. It precedes the QRS complex, which indicates ventricular depolarization. The R wave is part of this QRS complex. Conversely, the T wave represents ventricular repolarization, occurring after the ventricles have contracted. Understanding the sequence of these waves is crucial for interpreting heart activity on an ECG.

questions.text: Compared to blood, our lymph has:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, lymph does not consist of plasma without proteins. Lymph is a clear fluid similar to blood plasma but contains fewer proteins. It is composed of water, proteins, fats, and white blood cells. Blood plasma, on the other hand, is the liquid component of blood and contains water, proteins, fats, sugars, and other dissolved substances.

Correct. Lymph contains a higher concentration of white blood cells (WBCs) and no red blood cells (RBCs). WBCs are crucial for the immune system as they help the body fight infections, whereas RBCs, which transport oxygen, are absent in lymph.
No, this is incorrect. Lymph does not have more red blood cells (RBCs) and fewer white blood cells (WBCs) than blood. In fact, lymph does not contain any RBCs.

No, lymph does contain some plasma, though not as much as blood. Plasma is the liquid component that carries proteins, nutrients, hormones, and waste products. Lymph is similar to plasma but has fewer proteins and more white blood cells. RBCs, which are not found in lymph, are responsible for oxygen transport in the body.

questions.explanation: Lymph has more WBCs and no RBCs compared to blood. White blood cells (WBCs): WBCs are the main cells of the immune system. They help the body to fight off infection. Lymph contains a higher concentration of WBCs than blood does. Red blood cells (RBCs): RBCs are the cells that carry oxygen to the tissues. Lymph does not contain any RBCs.

questions.text: In humans, blood passes from the post-caval to the diastolic right atrium of the heart due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, the post caval does not pass blood to the right atrium of the heart due to suction pull. The post caval is a vein that drains blood from the lower body into the right atrium of the heart. Blood flows from the post caval to the right atrium due to gravity. The post caval is located below the right atrium, so the blood flows down into the right atrium by the force of gravity.

options.text: Pressure difference between the post caval and atrium
options.explanation: Yes, that is correct! Blood passes from the post caval to the diastolic right atrium of the heart due to the pressure difference between the two chambers. The post caval is a large vein that carries deoxygenated blood from the lower body to the right atrium of the heart. The right atrium is a chamber of the heart that receives deoxygenated blood from the post caval and the upper body. The pressure in the post caval is higher than the pressure in the right atrium. This pressure difference causes blood to flow from the post caval to the right atrium.The blood then flows from the right atrium to the right ventricle, which pumps it to the lungs to be oxygenated.

options.text: Pushing open of the venous valves
options.explanation: It is true that in humans, blood passes from the post cava to the diastolic right atrium of the heart due to the pushing open of the venous valves. The post cava is a large vein that carries deoxygenated blood from the lower body to the right atrium of the heart. The right atrium is the upper chamber of the right side of the heart. It receives deoxygenated blood from the post cava and the superior vena cava. The venous valves are located in the post cava and other veins that drain into the heart. These valves prevent backflow of blood when the heart contracts. When the heart relaxes, the venous valves open and allow blood to flow into the right atrium. The pushing open of the venous valves is caused by the difference in pressure between the blood in the post cava and the blood in the right atrium. The blood in the postcava is under higher pressure than the blood in the right atrium. This pressure difference causes the venous valves to open and allow blood to flow into the right atrium.
Yes, Both B and C are correct.

This pressure difference causes blood to flow from the post-caval to the right atrium. The blood then flows from the right atrium to the right ventricle, which pumps it to the lungs to be oxygenated. questions.text: A drop of each of the following is placed separately on four slides. Which of them will not coagulate?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Serum is the liquid part of blood after the removal of blood cells and clotting factors, including fibrinogen. It does not coagulate because it lacks the proteins necessary for clot formation.

options.text: Sample from the thoracic duct of the lymphatic system
options.explanation: While the lymph from the thoracic duct may have fewer clotting factors than blood, it still contains some fibrinogen and other components that can lead to coagulation under certain conditions.

options.text: Whole blood from the pulmonary vein
options.explanation: Whole blood contains all clotting factors, including fibrinogen, which are necessary for coagulation. Thus, it can clot when placed on a slide.
Not all options will prevent coagulation; only blood serum is incapable of coagulating due to the absence of fibrinogen.

questions.explanation: Out of the given options, only blood serum will not coagulate. Blood serum lacks clotting factors due to the removal of fibrinogen during the coagulation process, making it unable to form a clot on the slide. Options b) Sample from the thoracic duct of the lymphatic system and c) Whole blood from the pulmonary vein contain clotting factors and can coagulate if placed on a slide. Therefore, the correct answer is option a) Blood Serum.

questions.text: Which one of the following has an open circulatory system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Octopuses have a closed circulatory system with a chambered heart. However, unlike humans, their blood does not flow through arteries and veins. Instead, it flows through a network of vessels and sinuses. The blood in the octopus's circulatory system is blue because it contains a copper-based pigment called hemocyanin. Hemocyanin is similar to hemoglobin, the oxygen-carrying pigment in human blood, but it contains copper instead of iron.
Hirudinaria, or leeches, have a closed circulatory system.

Periplaneta, commonly known as the American cockroach, does have an open circulatory system. Open circulatory systems are found in insects and other invertebrates. In an open circulatory system, the blood flows freely in the body cavity and bathes the organs directly. There is no heart to pump the blood through the body. In Periplaneta, the blood flows through a series of tubes called hemocoel. The hemocoel is divided into two chambers: the pericardial sinus and the perivisceral sinus. The pericardial sinus surrounds the heart, and the perivisceral sinus surrounds the organs.
Of all mentioned above, only Periplaneta have open circulatory system.

questions.explanation: Periplaneta, commonly known as the American cockroach, does have an open circulatory system. Open circulatory systems are found in insects and other invertebrates. In an open circulatory system, the blood flows freely in the body cavity and bathes the organs directly. There is no heart to pump the blood through the body. In Periplaneta, the blood flows through a series of tubes called hemocoel. The hemocoel is divided into two chambers: the pericardial sinus and the perivisceral sinus. The pericardial sinus surrounds the heart, and the perivisceral sinus surrounds the organs.

questions.text: You are required to draw blood from a patient and to keep it in a test tube for analysis of blood corpuscles and plasma. You are also provided with the following four types of test tubes. Which of these you will not use for the purpose?

options.explanation: Calcium is crucial for blood clotting as it converts prothrombin into thrombin. The presence of calcium bicarbonate will promote clotting, making it unsuitable for analyzing blood corpuscles and plasma separately.
A normal test tube does not contain any substances that would affect the natural clotting process of blood.
Heparin is an anticoagulant that prevents blood from clotting, making it suitable for blood analysis.
Sodium oxalate acts as an anticoagulant by chelating calcium ions in the blood, which prevents clotting, suitable for blood analysis.

questions.explanation: Calcium plays a very important role in blood clotting by converting prothrombin into thrombin. The presence of calcium will remove heparin (blood anti-coagulant) and will promote blood clotting.

questions.text: The lymph serves to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Return the interstitial fluid to the blood
options.explanation: Lymph (also called tissue fluid in the intercellular spaces) is the colourless liquid found within the lymphatic system. An important function of lymph is to return interstitial fluid back to the blood. The interstitial fluid is the filtered form of the blood without the cellular components and plasma proteins.

options.text: Return the WBCs and RBCs to the lymph nodes
options.explanation: Lymph nodes filter lymph, so lymph can't return WBCs and RBCs to the lymph node. Moreover, lymph doesn't have RBCs.

questions.explanation: Lymph collects excess interstitial fluid, containing water, proteins, and wastes, and returns it to the blood, maintaining fluid balance in tissues.

questions.text: In veins, valves are present to check the backward flow of blood flowing at:

options.explanation: Veins carry blood from tissues to the heart, so they carry blood at low pressure. Valves are present in the veins to prevent backflow of blood due to force of gravity.
Veins carry blood from tissues to the heart, so they carry blood at low pressure. Valves are present in the veins to prevent backflow of blood due to force of gravity.

Arteries carry blood from the heart whose function is to pump blood at high pressure so that blood can reach each and every part of the body. questions.text: The neurogenic heart is the characteristic feature of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Humans possess a myogenic heart, meaning the cardiac muscle itself initiates the heart beat without nervous input, relying on a pacemaker like the SA node. Therefore, this option is incorrect, as the heart action is initiated internally.

Arthropods (e.g., insects) have a neurogenic heart, where the heart’s contraction is initiated by nerve impulses originating from a ganglion outside the heart muscle. These external nerves are necessary to stimulate the heart to beat.

Rabbits, like all mammals, have a myogenic heart where the heartbeat is initiated by specialized muscle tissue within the heart. This internal pacemaker system controls the rhythm of the heart contractions.

Rats, also being mammals, exhibit a myogenic heart, where the rhythm of contraction is regulated intrinsically by a specialized cardiac conduction system. The heart is able to contract even when isolated from the central nervous system.

questions.explanation: Arthropods have a neurogenic heart, where heartbeat is initiated by nerve impulses from a ganglion. In contrast, vertebrates like humans, rabbits, and rats have myogenic hearts, which beat due to specialized heart muscle tissue.

questions.text: The pacemaker in the heart is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The sino-atrial node, also known as the pacemaker of the heart, is responsible for initiating the electrical signals that regulate the heartbeat. It is located in the upper part of the right atrium and generates electrical impulses that spread through the atria, causing them to contract.

The atrioventricular node is located between the atria and the ventricles of the heart. It acts as a bridge between the electrical signals initiated by the sino-atrial node and the ventricles. The AV node delays the transmission of the electrical impulses, allowing the atria to fully contract before the ventricles are stimulated.

Purkinje fibres are specialized cardiac muscle fibres that rapidly transmit electrical impulses throughout the ventricles of the heart. They extend from the atrioventricular node and help coordinate the contraction of the ventricles, ensuring an efficient pumping action.

Papillary muscles are small, finger-like muscles located within the ventricles of the heart. They are attached to the chordae tendineae, which are fibrous cords that connect to the heart valves, specifically the mitral and tricuspid valves. The papillary muscles help anchor the valves and prevent them from inverting into the atria during ventricular contraction.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer is B) atrio-ventricular node (AVN), as it is responsible for transmitting the electrical signals from the atria to the ventricles, allowing for coordinated contractions of the heart chambers.

questions.text: Blood capillaries are made of:
questions.subject: Biology

Intestinal villi are tiny, finger-like projections made up of cells that line the entire length of your small intestine. Villi absorb nutrients from the food you eat and then shuttle them into your bloodstream so they can travel where they're needed.

The liver is a critical organ in the human body that is responsible for an array of functions that help support metabolism, immunity, digestion, detoxification, vitamin storage among other functions. It comprises around 2% of an adult's body weight.

questions.text: Which of the following explains the binding of inorganic components in structures found in erythrocytes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: CO with organic portion of haem
options.explanation: Carbon monoxide (CO) can bind to haemoglobin in a similar manner to oxygen, but it forms a much stronger bond with haemoglobin. This can lead to carbon monoxide poisoning, as it reduces the ability of haemoglobin to bind and transport oxygen effectively. The option is incorrect in saying that it binds to an "organic" portion because it binds to Fe that is inorganic.

options.text: CO2 with NH2 group of haemoglobin
options.explanation: Carbon dioxide (CO2) is mainly transported in the blood in the form of bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) rather than directly binding to haemoglobin.

The binding of the inorganic component in the structure found in erythrocytes (red blood cells) involves the binding of oxygen (O2) with the iron (Fe) component of the hemoglobin molecule. Haemoglobin is the protein present in red blood cells that is responsible for carrying oxygen from the lungs to the body's tissues.

options.text: H+ with COOH group of haemoglobin
options.explanation: Hydrogen ions (H+) do not directly bind to haemoglobin. However, they can influence the oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve and affect the affinity of haemoglobin for oxygen.

questions.explanation: Oxygen binds to the iron (Fe) in haem groups of haemoglobin, forming oxyhaemoglobin to transport oxygen from lungs to tissues.

questions.text: The type of blood cells that have a biconcave disc-like shape are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lymphocytes are a type of white blood cell that are responsible for the body's immune response. They are small, round cells with a nucleus and no granules.

Platelets are small, disc-shaped cell fragments that are essential for blood clotting. They are not true cells, as they do not have a nucleus or any other organelles. Platelets are produced in the bone marrow from megakaryocytes, which are large cells that divide repeatedly to produce platelets.

Monocytes are the largest type of white blood cell. They are round or oval-shaped cells with a granular cytoplasm. Monocytes are produced in the bone marrow and then circulate in the blood for a few days before moving to tissues, where they mature into macrophages.

This shape gives RBCs a large surface area to volume ratio, which is important for their role in carrying oxygen to the tissues.The biconcave disc shape of RBCs is also important for their ability to pass through the small capillaries in the tissues.

questions.explanation: RBCs are biconcave, increasing surface area for oxygen transport. They are anucleate, flexible for capillary passage, and live ~120 days before breakdown and iron recycling.

questions.text: Which one has the thicker walls than the others?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The walls of the right atrium are the thinnest of all the chambers of the heart. The left atrium has thicker walls than the right atrium, and the left ventricle has the thickest walls of all.

The left ventricle has the thickest walls of all the chambers of the heart. The right ventricle has the second thickest walls, followed by the left atrium and then the right atrium. The reason for this is that the left ventricle has to pump blood to all parts of the body, while the right ventricle only has to pump blood to the lungs. The left ventricle has to generate more force to pump blood against the higher resistance of the systemic circulation, so it has thicker walls.

The left ventricle has the thickest walls of all the heart chambers. This is because the left ventricle is responsible for pumping oxygenated blood to the rest of the body, which requires a lot of force. The right ventricle, on the other hand, is responsible for pumping deoxygenated blood to the lungs, which does not require as much force. As a result, the right ventricle has thinner walls than the left ventricle.

This is because the left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood to the entire body, while the right ventricle pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs. The left ventricle has to generate more force to pump blood against the higher resistance of the systemic circulation, so it needs thicker walls.The walls of the left ventricle are made up of cardiac muscle tissue, which is a type of muscle that is able to contract and relax very quickly. This allows the left ventricle to pump blood out of the heart at a high rate.

questions.explanation: The left ventricle has the thickest walls to generate enough force to pump oxygenated blood through the entire systemic circulation, unlike the right ventricle, which only pumps to the lungs.

questions.text: Cardiac output is increased by all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hypoxia, which is a low oxygen level in the tissues, can actually increase cardiac output. The body compensates for reduced oxygen availability by increasing heart rate and stroke volume to deliver more oxygenated blood to the tissues.
During exercise, cardiac output increases to meet the increased demand for oxygen and nutrients by the working muscles. This is achieved by an increase in both heart rate and stroke volume.
During sleep, cardiac output typically decreases compared to wakefulness. The body's metabolic demands are generally lower during sleep, resulting in a decreased need for an elevated cardiac output.

Pregnancy leads to an increase in cardiac output to meet the increased demands of the growing fetus and maternal tissues. Hormonal and physiological changes during pregnancy result in an increase in both heart rate and stroke volume.

questions.explanation: During sleep, cardiac output decreases due to lower metabolic demand, whereas exercise, hypoxia, and pregnancy increase cardiac output.

questions.text: Blood containing CO2 is:

This option is incorrect because the Autoimmune response refers to a malfunction of the immune system in which it mistakenly attacks healthy cells and tissues in the body as if they were foreign invaders.

questions.explanation: Transplantation of tissue/organ often fails due to nonacceptance by the patient’s body therefore, tissue matching and blood group matching are essential before undertaking any graft/transplant. When the immune system recognises the protein in the transplanted tissue or organ as foreign, it initiates cellular immunity. As a result of this, there is a rejection of transplanted organs. To suppress the immune response during transplantation, histocompatibility antigen and immunosuppressants play an important role.

questions.text: Short-lived immunity acquired from mother to foetus across the placenta or through the mother’s milk to the infant is categorised as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The use of vaccines, which stimulate the production of antibodies in the body, and making a person immune against the disease or infection, is called active immunity.

Short-lived immunity acquired from mothers to the foetus across the placenta or through the mother’s milk to the infant is categorised as passive immunity. Passive immunity, an acquired immunity, is resistance based on antibodies produced in another host. In this case, the foetus is not directly responsible for its body's immunity, but it becomes immunised by the mother’s milk across the placenta.
It is a type of adaptive (specific) immunity. It is mainly carried out by T lymphocytes (T cells).
It is the body’s first line of defense, present from birth.

questions.explanation: Short-lived immunity acquired from mothers to the foetus across the placenta or through the mother’s milk to the infant is categorised as passive immunity. Passive immunity, an acquired immunity, is resistance based on antibodies produced in another host. In this case, the foetus is not directly responsible for its body's immunity, but it becomes immunised by the mother’s milk across the placenta.

questions.text: In higher vertebrates, the immune system can distinguish between self-cells and non-self-cells. If this property is lost due to a genetic abnormality and it attacks self-cells, then it leads to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because Autoimmunity is a disorder of the body’s defense mechanisms in which an immune response is elicited against its own tissues, which are thereby damaged or destroyed. Autoimmunity may be caused due to genetic or environmental factors.
This option is incorrect because Active immunity is when the body manufactures its own antibodies when exposed to infectious particles.
This option is incorrect because an Attack on self-cells is not due to an allergic response.
This option is incorrect because Graft rejection is when the recipient's immune system attacks the donated graft and begins destroying grafted tissue or organ.

questions.explanation: Autoimmunity is a disorder of the body’s defense mechanisms in which an immune response is elicited against its own tissues, which are thereby damaged or destroyed. Autoimmunity may be caused due to genetic or environmental factors. This immune system dysfunction can result in a wide range of symptoms and health problems. There are more than 80 known autoimmune diseases, and they can affect virtually any part of the body.

questions.text: If you suspect a major deficiency of antibodies in a person, to which of the following would you look for confirmatory evidence?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because Haemocytes are essential components of the invertebrate immune system. They protect them from infections and maintain their health.

This option is correct because Serum globulins are proteins that include gamma globulins (antibodies) and a variety of enzymes and carrier/ transport proteins. The gamma fraction usually makes up the largest portion of the globulins, therefore antibody deficiency is mainly related to the low level of serum globulins.
This option is incorrect because Fibrinogen is a blood-clotting protein.
This option is incorrect because Serum albumin helps maintaining blood osmotic pressure.

questions.explanation: Serum globulins are proteins that include gamma globulins(antibodies) and a variety of enzymes and carrier/transport proteins. The specific profile of the globulins is determined by protein electrophoresis(SPEP), which separates the proteins according to size and charge. There are four major groups that can be identified: α-1 globulins, α-2 globulins, beta globulins, and gamma globulins. Once the abnormal group has been identified, further studies can determine the specific protein excess or deficit. Since the gamma fraction usually makes up the largest portion of the globulins, therefore antibody deficiency is mainly related to the low level of serum globulins.

questions.text: A grafted kidney may be rejected in a patient due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because passive immune response refers to immune response on the second exposure of the same antigen.
This option is incorrect because innate immune response is not involved in rejection.
This option is incorrect because a Grafted kidney may be rejected in a patient due to a cell-mediated immune response.

This option is correct because Cell-mediated immune response(CMIS) consists of T-lymphocytes. It reacts against transplants. Transplantation may result in the rejection of the transplanted organs. The immune system recognizes the protein in the transplanted tissue or organ as foreign and initiates cellular immunity against it.

questions.explanation: Cell-mediated immune response(CMIS) consists of T-lymphocytes. It reacts against transplants. Transplantation may result in the rejection of the transplanted organs. The immune system recognizes the protein in the transplanted tissue or organ as foreign and initiates cellular immunity against it.

questions.text: A cell coded protein that is formed in response to infection, with most animal viruses, is called :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Histones are proteins found in cell nuclei that help package and condense DNA. They are not directly involved in the immune response to viral infections.

Antibodies are proteins produced by the immune system in response to the presence of foreign substances (antigens) like viruses. Antibodies are essential for the immune system's ability to recognize and neutralize viruses, but they are not typically produced by infected cells.

Interferons are cell-coded proteins produced and released by infected cells as part of the innate immune response to viral infections. They play a crucial role in signaling neighboring cells to defend themselves against viral replication.

An antigen is a molecule that can stimulate an immune response, such as the production of antibodies. While antigens are associated with viruses and other pathogens, they are not the proteins directly produced in response to infection.

questions.explanation: Interferons are proteins that enhance a cell's resistance to viral attacks by unmasking genes responsible for synthesizing antiviral proteins. In humans, three groups of interferons have been discovered: 'a-interferons' from white blood cells, 'b-interferons' from connective tissue fibroblasts, and 'g-interferons' from lymphocytes.

questions.text: Which one of the following diseases is due to an allergic reaction?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Enteric fever, also known as typhoid fever, is caused by the bacterium Salmonella typhi and is not primarily an allergic reaction. It is a bacterial infection that affects the gastrointestinal system.

Skin cancer is not primarily due to an allergic reaction. It is primarily caused by the cumulative effects of ultraviolet (UV) radiation exposure from the sun or tanning beds, which can damage the DNA in skin cells and lead to cancer.
Goitre is typically caused by an iodine deficiency or other thyroid-related issues and is not primarily an allergic reaction. It results in the enlargement of the thyroid gland in the neck.

Hay fever, also known as allergic rhinitis, is due to an allergic reaction. It occurs when a person's immune system overreacts to allergens like pollen or dust, leading to symptoms such as sneezing, runny or stuffy nose, and itchy or watery eyes.

questions.explanation: Hay fever is an allergic reaction to the pollen of grasses, trees, and other plants. It is characterized by inflammation of the membrane lining the nose and sometimes the conjunctiva. The symptoms, including sneezing, a runny or blocked nose, and watering eyes, are a result of histamine released by mast cells.

questions.text: Pathogens inside the body are killed by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Antibodies are proteins that are produced by the immune system to fight infection. They bind to specific antigens on the surface of pathogens, which helps to mark them for destruction by the immune system. Immune system cells, such as phagocytes and B cells, also play a role in killing pathogens.
Phagocytes engulf and digest pathogens, while B cells produce antibodies.
Interferon is a protein that is produced by cells that are infected with viruses. It helps to protect other cells from being infected by the virus.

The immune system is a complex system that works to protect the body from infection. It uses a variety of mechanisms to kill pathogens, including antibodies, immune system cells, interferon, the complement system, natural killer cells, and cytokines.

questions.explanation: The immune system is a complex system that works to protect the body from infection. It uses a variety of mechanisms to kill pathogens, including antibodies, immune system cells, interferon, the complement system, natural killer cells, and cytokines.

questions.text: Interferons are synthesized in response to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Although the immune system does respond to Mycoplasma infections, interferons are not specifically synthesized in response to these bacteria.
Interferons are primarily involved in the response to viral infections and are not typically produced as a direct response to bacterial infections.
This is the correct answer. Interferons are signaling proteins synthesized by host cells in response to viral infections, playing a crucial role in the body's antiviral defense mechanisms.
Interferons are not generally synthesized in response to fungal infections; their primary role is in responding to viral threats.

questions.explanation: Interferons are synthesized in response to viral infections. Interferons are signaling proteins produced by host celis in response to the presence of viruses. They play a crucial role in the body's antivira tiviral defense mechanisms.

questions.text: The blood cancer is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Haemolysis refers to the breaking down of red blood cells, which can occur due to various causes, including certain medical conditions or exposure to toxins. While it involves red blood cells, it is not a type of blood cancer.
Haemophilia is a genetic disorder that affects the blood's ability to clot properly. It is not a form of blood cancer but rather a bleeding disorder caused by deficiencies in clotting factors.
Leukaemia is a type of cancer that affects the bone marrow and blood, leading to the uncontrolled production of abnormal white blood cells. It is indeed a form of blood cancer.
Thrombosis refers to the formation of blood clots within blood vessels, which can obstruct blood flow and cause various medical issues. It is not a type of blood cancer.

questions.explanation: The normal white blood cell (WBC) count ranges from 5,000 to 10,000 per cubic millimeter of blood. Leukemia, or blood cancer, is characterized by an abnormal increase in the WBC count, typically ranging from 20,000 to 1,000,000/mm3 due to their excessive production in the bone marrow. Hemolysis refers to the breakdown of red blood cells (RBCs). Hemophilia is a condition in which blood clots slowly, while thrombosis is the formation of blood clots inside blood vessels.

questions.text: Lysozyme that is present in perspiration, saliva and tears, destroys :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lysozyme is an antibacterial enzyme with natural antibiotic properties. Normally excreted in the tears, nasal mucus, milk, and saliva in most animals, lysozyme is part of the body’s first natural defense against bacteria and viruses. Lysozyme is an enzyme that degrades the polysaccharide protective coating on the surface of many bacteria and viruses (glycoprotein covering) to allow other enzymes and antibodies to find their appropriate attachment site. Most of the bacteria affected by lysozyme are not pathogenic. Lysozyme serves as a nonspecific innate opsonize by binding to the bacterial surface, reducing the negative charge, and facilitating phagocytosis of the bacterium before opsonins from the acquired immune system arrive at the scene. In other words, lysozyme makes it easier for phagocytic white blood cells to engulf bacteria.

Lysozymes are specific to the structure of bacterial cell walls, primarily targeting peptidoglycan, a component unique to bacteria. Viruses and fungi have different structures and are not susceptible to this mode of action.
Viruses are not considered as complete cells , as they lack cell wall. Since they lack cell wall , lysozymes have no target structure to act upon.
Fungi have complex cell wall. While lysozymes may affect some fungi, fungal cell walls are more complex than bacterial cell wall and may not be suspectable for lysozyme action.

questions.explanation: Lysozyme is an antibacterial enzyme with natural antibiotic properties. Normally excreted in the tears, nasal mucus, milk, and saliva in most animals, lysozyme is part of the body’s first natural defense against bacteria and viruses. Lysozyme is an enzyme that degrades the polysaccharide protective coating on the surface of many bacteria and viruses (glycoprotein covering) to allow other enzymes and antibodies to find their appropriate attachment site. Most of the bacteria affected by lysozyme are not pathogenic. Lysozyme serves as a nonspecific innate opsonin by binding to the bacterial surface, reducing the negative charge, and facilitating phagocytosis of the bacterium before opsonins from the acquired immune system arrive at the scene. In other words, lysozyme makes it easier for phagocytic white blood cells to engulf bacteria.

questions.text: Which one is cytotoxic?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These are a type of T lymphocytes that destroy virus-infected and cancer cells by releasing toxic chemicals like perforin and granzymes.
These are part of the innate immune system and kill infected or tumor cells without needing prior exposure or activation.
These are large white blood cells that engulf and digest bacteria, dead cells, and other debris; they are phagocytic, not cytotoxic.

CD8 T cells (also called cytotoxic T lymphocytes) directly kill infected or cancerous cells by releasing toxic substances like perforin and granzymes. NK cells (Natural Killer Cells) are also cytotoxic; they destroy virus-infected and tumor cells without needing prior activation or antigen recognition. So, both CD8 T cells and NK cells are cytotoxic because they can kill harmful cells in the body.

questions.explanation: CD8 T cells (also called cytotoxic T lymphocytes) directly kill infected or cancerous cells by releasing toxic substances like perforin and granzymes. NK cells (Natural Killer Cells) are also cytotoxic; they destroy virus-infected and tumor cells without needing prior activation or antigen recognition. So, both CD8 T cells and NK cells are cytotoxic because they can kill harmful cells in the body.

questions.text: Fever_:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fever actually enhances interferon production, which helps in antiviral defense.

options.text: Decreases the concentration of iron in the blood
options.explanation: This is correct. Fever reduces iron availability, which can inhibit bacterial growth as many bacteria require iron.
Fever enhances the activity of phagocytes, which are crucial for engulfing and destroying pathogens.

options.text: Decreases the reproduction of invading bacteria
options.explanation: While fever can slow bacterial reproduction, this option is more general and not as directly relevant as the correct answer regarding iron concentration.

questions.explanation: Fever is a common physiological response to infection, and it serves several immune-enhancing functions. One key aspect is reducing the concentration of iron in the blood. Many bacteria need iron to grow and multiply, and by sequestering iron, the body limits the resources available for bacterial proliferation, thus helping to control the infection. The other options are incorrect because fever actually promotes interferon production and enhances phagocytic activity, both of which are beneficial in fighting infections. Although fever can reduce bacterial reproduction, the direct mechanism related to iron is more precise.

questions.text: Lupus is a _ disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lupus is not characterized by a deficiency in hormones. It involves the immune system attacking the body's own tissues.
Lupus is an autoimmune disease, where the immune system mistakenly attacks healthy tissue in the body.
While vitamin D deficiency can affect immune function, it is not the cause of lupus.
This option is incorrect as lupus is indeed classified as a specific type of disease.

questions.explanation: Lupus is an autoimmune disease, meaning that the body's immune system attacks its own tissues and organs. This is different from a deficiency disease, where the body lacks a certain nutrient or hormone. The other options, such as hormonal deficiency and vitamin D deficiency, involve a lack of specific substances in the body and do not accurately describe lupus. Therefore, the correct answer is that lupus is an autoimmune disease.

questions.text: Allergy is caused due to excessive secretion of _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: IgE is the antibody primarily responsible for mediating allergic reactions. It binds to allergens and triggers the degranulation of mast cells and basophils, leading to the release of histamine and other inflammatory mediators.
IgG is the most prevalent antibody in the bloodstream and is crucial for long-term immunity and fighting infections. It does not play a direct role in allergic reactions.
IgD is primarily found on the surface of B cells and is involved in the initiation of B cell activation. It does not have a significant role in allergic responses.
IgM is the first antibody produced in response to an infection and is effective in the early stages of immune response. It is not associated with allergy symptoms.

questions.explanation: IgE antibodies trigger histamine release, leading to allergic reactions.

questions.text: If both parents of a person are allergic, the chance of allergy in that person is_?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This percentage is too low given both parents are allergic.
This is an unlikely low probability given the genetic predisposition.
This is the correct probability based on genetic studies showing higher risk when both parents have allergies.
Although possible, this percentage is lower than studies suggest when both parents are allergic.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 60%. Genetic studies indicate that when both parents have allergies, the probability of their child developing allergies significantly increases due to inherited genetic factors. The 30% and 15% options are too low because they don't account for the increased risk from both parents. The 40% option is closer but still underestimates the likelihood as suggested by genetic research.

questions.text: The outer secretion of the body contain_.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: IgE is primarily associated with allergic reactions and defense against parasitic infections, not typically found in outer secretions.

IgA is the main immunoglobulin found in mucosal areas such as the gut, respiratory tract, and urogenital tract, as well as in saliva, tears, and breast milk. It plays a crucial role in mucosal immunity and is the correct answer.
IgM is the first antibody produced in response to an infection and is primarily found in the blood and lymphatic fluid, not in outer secretions.
IgD is found in small amounts in the blood and is primarily involved in the initiation of immune responses, not in outer secretions.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is IgA, as it is the immunoglobulin predominantly found in mucosal secretions and plays a vital role in protecting body surfaces exposed to external environments. IgA helps prevent the attachment and invasion of pathogens through mucosal surfaces. In contrast, IgE is mainly involved in allergic responses, IgM is the first responder in systemic infections, and IgD has a limited role in the initiation of immune responses.

questions.text: Which one is a live vaccine?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Sabin vaccine is a live attenuated vaccine, which means it contains a weakened form of the poliovirus. It is given orally as the Oral Polio Vaccine (OPV). Because the virus can still multiply a little, it provides strong and long-lasting immunity against polio.

The Salk vaccine is an inactivated or killed vaccine that contains the poliovirus in a non-living form. It is given by injection as the Inactivated Polio Vaccine (IPV). Since the virus is dead, it is very safe but produces weaker immunity compared to the Sabin vaccine.

A toxoid is a vaccine made from a bacterial toxin that has been inactivated so it cannot cause harm. Examples include the tetanus toxoid and diphtheria toxoid. These vaccines work by training the body to fight against the harmful effects of toxins rather than the bacteria themselves.
This is not correct here, because Sabin is clearly a live vaccine.

questions.explanation: The Sabin vaccine is a live attenuated vaccine, which means it contains a weakened form of the poliovirus. It is given orally as the Oral Polio Vaccine (OPV). Because the virus can still multiply a little, it provides strong and long-lasting immunity against polio.

questions.text: Most abundant WBC are?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Macrophages are a type of white blood cell involved in detecting, engulfing and destroying pathogens, but they are not the most abundant type.
Neutrophils are the most abundant type of white blood cells and are the first responders to microbial infection.
Monocytes are a type of white blood cell that differentiates into macrophages and dendritic cells, but they are not the most abundant.
CD8 cells are a subtype of T cells, which play a role in the immune response, but they are not the most abundant type of white blood cell.

questions.explanation: Neutrophils are the most abundant type of white blood cells (WBCs) in the human body, comprising about 50-70% of all WBCs. They are crucial in the body's defense against infections, as they are the first to arrive at an infection site to begin microbial eradication. In contrast, macrophages, monocytes, and CD8 cells, while important for immune functions, do not exist in as high numbers as neutrophils. Macrophages and monocytes play roles in engulfing and digesting pathogens and dead cells, while CD8 cells are involved in directly killing infected cells, but these are not as numerous as neutrophils.

questions.text: Dermis contain _ protein.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Keratin is a protein found in the epidermis, hair, and nails, not primarily in the dermis.
Collagen is the predominant protein in the dermis, providing structural support and strength to the skin.
Hemoglobin is a protein found in red blood cells, responsible for oxygen transport, not a component of the dermis.
This option is incorrect because not all the mentioned proteins are present in the dermis.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Collagen. Collagen is the main structural protein found in the dermis of the skin, providing it with strength and elasticity. Keratin is found in the outer layer of the skin, hair, and nails, not primarily in the dermis. Hemoglobin is not found in the dermis; it is a protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen. The option All is incorrect because not all these proteins are present in the dermis.

questions.text: Which cells regulate the opening and closing of the stoma?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Neutrophil cells are part of the immune system in animals, not involved in regulating plant stomata.
Basophil cells are a type of white blood cell in animals, unrelated to plant stoma regulation.
Mesophyll cells are involved in photosynthesis but do not regulate stomatal opening and closing.
Guard cells are specialized plant cells that regulate the opening and closing of stomata by changing their shape and turgor pressure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is guard cells. These are specialized cells located on either side of a stoma. By altering their turgor pressure, they control the opening and closing of the stomatal pore, thus regulating gas exchange and water loss in plants. Neutrophil and basophil cells are types of white blood cells involved in animal immune responses, not related to plant functions. Mesophyll cells are responsible for photosynthesis but do not play a role in stomatal regulation.

questions.text: In a standard ECG, which one of the following alphabets is the correct representation of the respective activity of the human heart?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The P wave does not indicate the start of systole. It represents atrial depolarization, which occurs before the ventricles contract. Systole is associated with the QRS complex.
The T wave signifies ventricular repolarization, not the end of diastole. Diastole is the period of relaxation and filling of the heart chambers, following systole.
The P wave in an ECG represents the depolarization of the atria, which is the electrical activity causing the atria to contract.
The R wave is part of the QRS complex, representing ventricular depolarization. Ventricular repolarization is indicated by the T wave.

questions.explanation: The P wave in a standard ECG represents the depolarization of the atria. This marks the electrical activity that causes the atria to contract and push blood into the ventricles. The P wave is a small, rounded wave at the beginning of the ECG tracing. It precedes the QRS complex, which indicates ventricular depolarization. The R wave is part of this QRS complex. Conversely, the T wave represents ventricular repolarization, occurring after the ventricles have contracted. Understanding the sequence of these waves is crucial for interpreting heart activity on an ECG.

questions.text: Arteries are best defined as the vessels which:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Carry blood away from the heart
options.explanation: Arteries are best defined as the vessels which carry blood away from the heart. They have thick walls and are elastic, which allows them to withstand the pressure of the blood being pumped by the heart. Arteries branch into smaller and smaller vessels as they get further away from the heart.

options.text: Break up into capillaries, then form veins
options.explanation: Arteries break up into capillaries, which are the smallest blood vessels in the body. Capillaries are so small that red blood cells can only pass through them one at a time. Capillaries allow the exchange of oxygen and nutrients between the blood and the tissues. The capillaries then reunite to form veins, which carry blood back to the heart.
Visceral arteries branch off of the abdominal aorta and supply blood to the stomach, intestines, liver, spleen, pancreas, and kidneys.

The arteries that supply oxygenated blood to different organs include the coronary arteries, pulmonary arteries, cerebral arteries, carotid arteries, subclavian arteries, abdominal aorta, and femoral arteries.

questions.explanation: Arteries are defined by their direction of flow—always carrying blood away from the heart, irrespective of oxygenation.

questions.text: Oxygenated blood is supplied to all body parts from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, oxygenated blood is not supplied to all body parts from the right atrium. The right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from the body and passes it to the right ventricle.
No, the left atrium receives oxygenated blood from the lungs but does not supply it to the body. It passes the blood to the left ventricle.
No, the right ventricle pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs, not to the body.
Yes, the left ventricle is responsible for pumping oxygenated blood to all parts of the body through the aorta.

questions.explanation: The left ventricle is the main pumping chamber of the heart, responsible for distributing oxygenated blood to the body. It receives oxygenated blood from the left atrium and pumps it through the aorta. The other options are incorrect because the right atrium and right ventricle handle deoxygenated blood, while the left atrium only transfers oxygenated blood to the left ventricle without directly supplying it to the body.
